heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CIII
card_summary: This chunk is part of Campanini's extended introductory essay cataloguing the kabbalistic sources cited by Zorzi in the De Harmonia Mundi. It annotates entries 17 through 30 of Zorzi's catalogus auctorum, identifying figures such as Mosheh Botarel (Pater Mosi Butriel), Sa'adyah Ga'on (Sebadia), Nachmanide (Moses Gerundinensis filius Nehemani), Yosef Gikatilla (Iosef Cicatiglia), Menachem Recanati (Manahen Ricanati), Yehudah ha-Nassi (Iehuda Nagid), Yosef Albo, Mosheh de León, Bachyah ben Asher (Rabi Bahaie), Ya'aqov and Yitzchaq ha-Kohen of Soria, and Todros ha-Lewi Abulafia (Rabi Tedacus Levi). Campanini carefully traces whether each attribution derives from Johannes Reuchlin's De arte cabalistica, from Giovanni Pico della Mirandola's circle, or from Zorzi's independent reading, exposing numerous scribal errors, misreadings, and second-hand transmissions. The section highlights Zorzi's substantial dependence on Reuchlin, while also identifying cases—notably Bachyah ben Asher and Gikatilla—where independent acquaintance appears more likely.
essay:
# SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CIII (pp. 103–108): Zorzi's Kabbalistic Catalogus — Entries 17–30

## Overview

This section of Campanini's introductory essay continues the systematic philological identification of the kabbalistic authors named in Zorzi's *catalogus auctorum* in the *De Harmonia Mundi*. Working entry by entry, Campanini assesses whether each name derives from Johannes Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* (1517), from the Pico della Mirandola circle (via Flavio Mitridate's Latin translations), or from Zorzi's direct reading. The dominant finding across almost all entries is that Zorzi leans heavily on Reuchlin, often reproducing his errors and misreadings, while independent familiarity can be demonstrated only in a handful of cases.

## Entry 17: Pater Mosi Butriel — Mosheh Botarel

Zorzi's phrase "Pater Mosi Butriel" is clarified by Campanini as a reference to the father of Mosheh ben Yitzchaq Botarel, whose *Commento al Sefer Yetzirah* Zorzi used extensively. The odd construction — citing the father without a proper name — derives from Botarel's own commentary, where he alludes ambiguously to his father as a source of certain teachings:

> Con questa designazione, a dispetto della virgola che si legge nelle edizioni a stampa del De Harmonia mundi, Zorzi allude al più volte ricordato Commento al Sefer Yetzirah di Mosheh ben Yitzchaq Botarel, che egli conobbe e del quale fece, come si è mostrato in queste pagine, ampio uso.

<cite>p. 103</cite>

## Entry 18: Sebadia — Sa'adyah Ga'on

Zorzi's "Sebadia" is identified as Sa'adyah ben Yosef, the great Gaonic philosopher, known in Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* as "Saadia" and credited there with the *Sefer ha-emunot* (*Emunot we-de'ot*). Campanini argues that the corrupted spelling ("Sebadia" vs. "Saadia") may result from a scribal exchange of the Hebrew letters *'ayin* and *bet*. Whether Zorzi had direct knowledge of Sa'adyah's commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah* is deemed improbable, since Zorzi conspicuously omits him from the dedicated section on *Sefer Yetzirah* commentators.

## Entry 19: Moses Gerundinensis filius Nehemani — Nachmanide

Nachmanide (Mosheh ben Nachman, Ramban) is identified through Zorzi's formula, which closely resembles Reuchlin's phrasing in the *De arte cabalistica*: "[...] et insignes commentarii Ramban, nam ita collective nominatur Rabi Moyses filius Nehmani super arcana legis quem appellatis Gerundensem." Campanini finds Zorzi's added clarification — "Ramban in fine per n" — slightly redundant and slightly out of place, which reinforces dependence on Reuchlin. An alternative or supplementary path through Pico della Mirandola's *Heptaplus* (which uses the exact phrase "Moses Gerundinensis") and Abraham Abulafia's *Sitre Torah* (as identified by Wirszubski) is noted but cannot be confirmed.

## Entry 20: Iosef Cicatiglia — Yosef Gikatilla

Unlike most other entries, Zorzi's citation of Gikatilla does not appear to derive from Reuchlin, since Zorzi neither spells the name as Reuchlin does nor attributes to Gikatilla the *Sha'are Orah* (*Porta lucis*), which Reuchlin mentions explicitly. Instead, in his *Problemata* Zorzi demonstrates knowledge of another Gikatilla work, the *Ginnat egoz* (which Zorzi renders as *hortulus nucum*):

> Nei Problemata Zorzi mostra di conoscere un'altra opera di Gikatilla, ovvero il Ginnat egoz, da lui tradotto con hortulus nucum.

<cite>p. 104</cite>

Campanini raises but cannot confirm the possibility that Zorzi accessed Gikatilla through the Pico circle, where Gikatilla — a student of Avraham Abulafia — was well known from Flavio Mitridate's Latin translations.

## Entry 21: Manahen Ricanati — Menachem Recanati

The Italian kabbalist Menachem Recanati is cited under a spelling that diverges from Reuchlin's "Mnahem Racanat," suggesting some independence. Campanini notes Recanati's central importance for Pico della Mirandola's Christian Kabbalah but judges that direct acquaintance with Recanati's works by Zorzi cannot be demonstrated. A commentary on prayers listed separately in Zorzi's *catalogus* may be Recanati's (also translated by Flavio Mitridate), but Zorzi does not link it to the author.

## Entry 22: Iehuda Nagid — Yehudah ha-Nassi

The appearance of Yehudah ha-Nassi (the *Nasi*, or Prince, compiler of the Mishnah) in a list of kabbalistic authors is identified as anomalous. The only Christian kabbalistic context in which this figure appears as a kabbalah source is Pablo de Heredia's *Epistula de secretis*, where he is called *Rabbenu ha-qadosh* ("the holy"). Campanini considers it more likely that Zorzi follows Reuchlin, who presents Yehudah as a link in the chain of oral transmission rather than as a kabbalah author per se. Moshe Idel's alternative hypothesis — that Zorzi's source is Abraham Abulafia's epistle *We-zo't li-Yehudah*, which mentions a "Yehudah ha-naggid" (to be emended to "he-chasid," i.e., Yehudah the Pious) — is discussed but judged unproven.

## Entry 23: Rabi Ioseph Albo — Yosef Albo

Both Reuchlin and Zorzi miscategorize Yosef Albo, the 14th–15th century Spanish philosopher and polemicist (*Sefer ha-'iqqarim*), as a kabbalistic writer. Campanini notes pointedly that neither Reuchlin nor Zorzi appears to have actually read the *Sefer ha-'iqqarim*, since both fail to register its anti-Christian polemical content.

## Entry 24: Rabi Moses de Leone — Mosheh de León

Mosheh de León is presented without extended comment; Campanini notes only that by the nineteenth century he had been recognized as the author of the *Sefer ha-Zohar*, the most important work in kabbalistic literature, in addition to works that had long circulated under his name. Which of his works Zorzi may have consulted remains impossible to determine.

## Entry 25: Rabi Ama — Todros ha-Lewi Abulafia

The name "Rabi Ama" (also "Sofer Ama," the scribe) is traced to an error in Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*, where this figure is credited with the kabbalistic work *Sha'ar ha-razim* ("Porta dei segreti"), a mystical commentary on Psalm 19. The actual author of this work is Todros ha-Lewi Abulafia.

## Entry 26: Rabi Bahaie — Bachyah ben Asher

This entry is notable as one of the few where Campanini identifies probable direct acquaintance independent of Reuchlin (who does not mention Bachyah at all). Zorzi accurately reports an interpretation from Bachyah ben Asher's *Be'ur 'al ha-Torah* concerning the precious stone called *Tarshish*, which Bachyah glosses with the Hebrew term for *chrysolitus* (the Latin transparent through a corrupted Hebrew spelling):

> Zorzi, infatti, riferisce accuratamente un'interpretazione che si legge nel Commento di Bachyah, a proposito della pietra preziosa chiamata Tarshish che Bachyah interpreta con il termine [...] evidente storpiatura del latino chrysolitus.

<cite>p. 105</cite>

## Entries 27–29: Sefer Yetzirah Commentators (Ya'aqov Kohen, Rabi Moses, Rabi Isahac)

Zorzi groups the next entries as commentators on the *Sefer Yetzirah*. "Iahacob Cohen" is Ya'aqov Kohen of Soria; the absence of citations from his works points toward Reuchlin as the source (Scholem had suggested Reuchlin knew a now-lost commentary by Ya'aqov Kohen). "Rabi Moses" is too generic for certain identification — Campanini suggests Mosheh Botarel or Nachmanide as the most probable candidates. "Rabi Isahac" may be Isaac the Blind, Yitzchaq ben Shemu'el (cited by Botarel), or most likely Yitzchaq ben Ya'aqov ha-Kohen (brother of Ya'aqov Kohen of Soria), credited by Reuchlin with a now-lost *Sefer Yetzirah* commentary. The parallel citation in Paolo Ricci's *Isagoge* (Pavia, 1509) of a "Rabi Ysac" adds further complexity.

## Entry 30: Rabi Tedacus Levi — Todros ha-Lewi Abulafia (again, via acrostic error)

This entry is traced by Campanini to a famous misreading by Reuchlin of a manuscript acrostic. Scholem (article of 1927, documented evidence published 1969) identified the source as manuscript Halberstam 444 at the Jewish Theological Seminary, New York, where an acrostic on fol. 1r encodes the name Todros ben Yosef ha-Lewi Abulafia. Reuchlin misread the Italian-script *resh* as a *dalet*, producing "Tedacus Levi" instead of the correct reading. Zorzi, following Reuchlin, perpetuates this ghost name:

> Reuchlin avrebbe interpretato la non chiara grafia italiana del manoscritto, che reca accanto all'acrostico la sua soluzione [...] scambiando la resh per una [dalet].

<cite>pp. 107–108</cite>

## Significance for Zorzi's Sources

The overall picture that emerges from entries 17–30 is that Zorzi's *catalogus* is substantially indebted to Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* for both identification and spelling of kabbalistic authorities. Independent knowledge can be demonstrated or plausibly argued only for Bachyah ben Asher and Gikatilla's *Ginnat egoz*. The Pico circle and Flavio Mitridate's Latin translations represent a secondary, partly inaccessible stratum. Several ghost names and misattributions (Rabi Ama, Tedacus Levi, Ioseph Albo as kabbalist) pass uncorrected from Reuchlin into Zorzi, testifying to the limits of direct Hebrew reading in the Venetian Franciscan milieu.
